speaker: interviewer
text: --1995. Please continue.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I-- we were talking about my sister. So I was only very happy that she could go to the States. Oh, I'm sorry, I made a mistake. I was-- we were not there. Was that correct?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, actually we were. That's OK. We were at Chateau de Chabannes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my sister went to the States. So I stayed in Chabannes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why couldn't you go with her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the reason I couldn't go with her-- because they only were taking children up to 11, 11 and 1/2. My sister was short six months. She was almost 12, but the director felt he could get her through. So, as a matter of fact, she celebrated her 12th birthday in Lisbon, Portugal, before they were boarding the ship to New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was, at that time, that was-- 15 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you afraid to send her there?

speaker: interviewee
text: They couldn't-- they didn't take the older-- the older kids. They only took the younger ones because it was very difficult to get them-- it was the Quaker organization who did that. So-- but I felt better about it because now I could take care of myself.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then I don't know if I mentioned it to you-- I was asked-- I had a girlfriend in Chabannes, and I was asked if I would change homes because my girlfriend, [? Angel, ?] had a younger brother and sister living in the other home. And, again, he told me, if you don't like it in the other home, you can come back any time you want back to Chabannes. So I presume that when I came to Chabannes, I must have been registered with the what we call the [? mairie, ?] or the city hall, of all the people that lived there. So I went to Chateau de la Hille, and I really liked it very, very much. Where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was is in southern France near Papillon, right near the Spanish border in the Pyrenees. Right near there. And it was lovely. It was very nice. It was sort of primitive, but it was very, very nice. And they had a lady there. She was one of the directors. We had a man and a woman. I don't remember the gentleman. But the lady, her name was Mrs.-- Miss Neff, N-E-F-F, from Switzerland, too. As a younger woman, she was Dr. Albert Schweitzer's nurse in Lambarene, in Congo, in Africa. So she would always tell us stories about Dr. Schweitzer. We would sit. And it was a very nice home. And then we could hear the news that the Germans were coming closer to southern France.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what year this was?

speaker: interviewee
text: That must have been 1942, something like that. You know, dates-- it seems like things happen every day, so it almost felt like 10 years. Maybe it was only one year. I don't remember. And so we started, in the beginning, to sleep in the attic because we could hear the Germans coming close. So after about two weeks, the director decided, well, maybe they're not going to come to our home. Anyway, we went back into the dormitory, our own dorm. And what we did, we had two of the older guys watching on the roof to alert us if the Germans were coming.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people lived in that home?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were, oh, maybe-- altogether maybe 60 or 80. Between 60 and 80 kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the age ranges?

speaker: interviewee
text: The age group-- there were small ones. There was a whole group. There were maybe 20. I don't remember exactly. And the rest were 14 and up-- older.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the one day we were kind of lax with watching, the Germans arrived, middle of the night in our home. And we had to open the doors for them, and we had to get dressed. They stayed in each room and watched us, how we got dressed.

speaker: interviewee
text: They walked us to the toilets. I mean, they wouldn't leave us alone. Afraid that we would run away or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe what they looked like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they wore Nazi uniform, military. You know, they were the regular Nazi outfits. And so we had to go into the courtyard, and they told us that boys on one side and girls on the other. So each one was allowed one little suitcase, so I had my suitcase standing there. And they were calling alphabetically names. And then all of a sudden, I heard the name Wolf being called, but my name was Weinberg, and it's before.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was just trying to walk over to say, I didn't hear my name. The director noticed that, and he came sort of in back of me and he said, leave your suitcase-- in French-- leave your suitcase, get into the house, go into the bathroom, and lock the door.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was a lot of commotion, so they didn't pay too much attention to me. And I walked into the house and locked myself in the bathroom until I could hear [? the camion, ?] the trucks, leave.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in the bathroom?

speaker: interviewee
text: I must have been maybe two hours or more, because until they get everybody on the truck, and all this commotion. And then somebody knocked on my door, and it was another person. Because both directors decided, told the Germans, that they're going with them to the camp. Ultimately, they had to go back home. The directors didn't want to leave their charge alone going to camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the children were being sent to--

speaker: interviewee
text: To a camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know which camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Camp Gurs, I think, G-U-R-S. I don't remember, but that's what I heard.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like a concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In southern France, not too far from where we were. Not very far from there is what I heard. I've never seen it. I don't know. All I know that later, after the war, my uncle told me he was there for a little while and he escaped from there. And then the directors both had to come back because they wouldn't let them into the camp. And at that point, they were in contact with an underground organization. In France, they're called the Marquis.

speaker: interviewer
text: But do you know why your name wasn't on the list?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I presume that my name wasn't on the list because I think I was registered in Chabannes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the first home.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the first home. Because they were picked up there. My friends were picked up from there and were sent to camps, to Auschwitz and to Buchenwald and different camps, from Chabannes. So a lucky star, I was not there. That I was not registered in the other camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: I forgot to tell you something. Just, you know, how things come back to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1936, I visited my aunt. I was a little girl. And the Olympic games were on in Berlin. And I remember they took me to the stadium, and I saw Hitler sitting there. And I remember seeing Jesse Owens. They told me who he was, an American, running. And I was just fascinated by that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, the limousine-- I saw Hitler's limousine, and everybody was running after him, and I thought he was like a movie star. So I tried to ask, can I go and see him? My aunt said, no, no, no, no, no, you can't go and see him. But I thought it was sort of interesting because I still-- I was never hurt at that point, so maybe I didn't think Hitler was that bad. How old were you in '36?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1936, I was 12 years old. No, 11 years old-- whatever. I don't remember from day to day. But it was-- you know, because I still, personally, had no problem yet. So Hitler was just the Chancellor-- to me, like a president. I didn't know who he was really. So that was sort of funny thinking, you know, when you hadn't been hurt at this point. Now going back to Chateau de la Hille. So I was sent to the underground. They gave me a name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who sent you to the underground?

speaker: interviewee
text: From that home, from Chateau de la Hille, this director. He said, you're going to go take a bus, and you're going to this and this town, and somebody will pick you up. And you will go to this family, live with this family, and you're going to be like an au pair with them. And you just do-- whatever they tell you, you do.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went there. Where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Montauban in, sort of, Central France, too. And the family, they were very nice. And I stayed there for a time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember their name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't remember the name at all. And so I worked as an au pair. I helped the maid, in essence. And then, I was sent-- from that point on, it's almost like a blank. I was sent to so many different places. Apparently--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who sent you?

speaker: interviewee
text: To different families, to different places. Because the way the underground works, you cannot stay in one place too long because they were more afraid of the French, the Vichy government, at that point, who were very much pro-German. They did not want to take chances in keeping people in places too long. Even some time I was told that I have to take a train ride, I remember once, to Toulouse. And they told me to try to sit with Germans if you can. They have these compartments on a train, and if you see an empty seat in a compartment where there are Germans in there, just go in there and sit and, kind of, listen what they have to say. Apparently there were certain areas where the underground wanted to know things-- maybe where they were going or about the soldiers. And I used to do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: They didn't know you were Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they didn't know. And I had my French papers. But the underground, what they do to you, they give you a whole new birth certificate, a whole new identification card, and they drill you. You have to, sort of, block out your past life. You are now-- my name was changed to Florence Suzanne Van Sant. And they really work with you because if, God forbid, the French looked at your papers and-- I was more worried about the French looking at my papers than the Germans. The Germans didn't know. They saw my papers. They didn't pay any attention They didn't know [? whether they were ?] real or false. I was-- we were more afraid of the French government, the pro-German, the Vichy government. But so far, I had lucked out. You know, I had no problem.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember who gave you the papers, who drilled you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, you really never get to know the people for real because they really don't-- you're not supposed to know so much about their name. Because if, God forbid, you get caught, and they will say, who gave you these papers? You know these papers are not right. And, you know, they can-- maybe I'll break down and I would give somebody's name of where I was recently. So they don't. You just have to learn to be a different person. So that's what I did. And you know, I think back now-- when you're younger, you do a lot of things under normal circumstances you would-- you wouldn't do.

speaker: interviewee
text: I got to drink a little water.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyhow, I went to one family that was sort of interesting-- a woman. She was a widow. Her husband was a doctor, and she had a lovely home near Bordeaux someplace. She also had a home up in the mountains, which I found out later that she hid Jewish families up in her mountain home. And in that town, there were a lot of German soldiers. And our house was sort of on a railroad, a bridge. And there were soldiers walking by our house all the time. And I could hear-- [COUGHS] I could hear them speak different languages. And they were speaking Polish and Russian. And they were really taken by the Germans and put into their army. So-- and when a German officer would hear that, they could get shot for that. They had to speak German. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like some more water?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: I have some right here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Please, thank you. My throat is so dry.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in the mornings I would get up, and there was a German soldier always nearby. And he would help me make the fire, and not knowing who I was. And we got along just fine because I had to pretend. I played a pretend game. Can you describe that-- how you pulled it off?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, no, I was just a French person, and I didn't pay any attention to him. You kind of have to do these things. It's like you're acting a part. You have to do it. It's all part of your survival. You almost go through survival training, and that's what you do.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, as a matter of fact, I remember the lady-- we would listen to the radio at night, shortwave radio, and there were one or two of the soldiers would come in and listen to it. And then I found out she helped them escape from the German soldiers because they were not Germans, and they didn't want any part being in a German uniform. They were Ukraines and Poles and Russians, and they wanted to go back. So she helped them. And I feel, when I think back, she took chances, this lady, hiding Jews. Do you remember her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I don't remember her name. I can picture her. She was a very tall, dark-haired, attractive lady, and she had three children. And I don't remember her name. And it really angers me at time that I didn't keep all these names, but I just can't remember her name. Then I was sent to another town, to Montauban, which is also Central France. And the war got really bad at this point. And it was the Germans were starting now to lose the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year? What was the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was '43, late '43, '44-- something like that. '43. In Europe, anyway, it was-- and the Germans were getting terrible. They were picking people up left and right. We had to watch hangings. They were picking up people from the underground. They were catching them. And they would hang people right in the streets. And you had gallows put up in the streets, and they let the civilians-- we had to watch this.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Montauban?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Montauban. Now, one of the persons-- I don't know who-- took me to the bishop, the Catholic bishop in Montauban. And he explained to me, and he says for my sake, it would be best that I would enter a convent. And the convent was run by the Carmelite sisters. As you know, the Carmelite sisters do not go out. They're cloistered nuns.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I decided-- I said, OK, I'll do that. And I went into the convent, and the only one that knew that I was Jewish was one of the priests and Mother Superior. So I was sent in as a Protestant.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the convent also was almost like a girl's home for wayward girls. They would pick up young prostitutes, and they would bring them into the convents and--

speaker: interviewer
text: And they would live there in the convent. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

words.text: And they would live there in the convent.

speaker: interviewee
text: --try to educate them. They lived there, yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I had to, kind of, fit in this milieu. And, you know, for some reason there was one girl there and I really liked her. I didn't know anything about her background or anything, but she and I, kind of-- we got along. We sat next to each other to eat. And she was forever getting punished, and then I would kind of get angry at the nuns, you know, because she would get punished. But it's nothing I could say too much. What would she get punished for?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I really don't know. Things that she-- they claimed she didn't behave. When you were walking around the courtyard, you could not walk in twos. You had to walk in threes or fours or five because they were afraid you were going to plan an escape or something else. And this girl was very defiant. And I think I was sort of feisty, too, in those days. I was defiant, so I was going to show them. You know, they're not going to push me around. And she and I, we kind of plotted those things-- that we were going to work together. And then we saw several things what went on within the cloisters. You know, it wasn't very nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe those things?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we observed the others, the nuns, and the illicit affairs. And the priests would come. But one day, Mother Superior decided it was not very good for me. I had to become a Catholic now. So I said, fine. I became a Catholic, and I went through the conversion. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe the conversion? Did you have to pray?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. I got a rosary, I was baptized. And, yeah, it was through a whole conversion I went. But again, I had to play the game. I had to go along because I was determined I was going to survive this war. And the interesting part was there was a-- they had different categories within the convent. They had girls that were maybe prostitutes or thieves or God knows what, and they wanted to become a nun. They had to be put in one category. Then the women that would come with not much of a dowry. There would be one other type-- they would do another type of work. They would do more labor, kitchen, washing, I mean, the laundry and cooking and cleaning. Then the girls or the women with a large dowry would come. And I witnessed one day a wedding. A young woman becoming a nun, and how she gets married and coming right in a wedding dress. And the Bishop. And she gets her hair cut off, and she is now-- we called them mothers. And there was a young nun there, and she already wore her black outfit, which means she's been there for now a few years. Because you get five years for final vows. And she fascinated me, this one nun. And one day, I-- her room was behind my bunk. And I saw her combing her hair. And you're not supposed to have long hair under your outfit. And the next day I asked her-- I says, you have long hair under there. You're not supposed to do that. And she says, oh, it's nothing. She kind of pushed aside. Now the day of liberation arrives, and we were all looking out of the window. And the soldiers, the Americans, came through our town and were marching, and everybody's excited.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she is so excited. She looks out. She made a remark-- oh, look, isn't that a handsome soldier? So finally, I thought, no, she's not for real. Within about a week, she got a letter. And I saw her running through my dorm trying to go into her room. And I says, Mother, you're so happy. You have good news?

speaker: interviewee
text: She says, yeah, my cousin survived the war. He was a soldier.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, Mother, your cousin, or was it your husband, maybe, or your boyfriend or something?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then she told me that she was Jewish from Belgium, and she had made a pact with her fiancee that she would be able to go to France [INAUDIBLE], she would become a nun, never thinking the war would last five or more years. And when he would come back, he would find her. And that's exactly what she did. And he found her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but I can picture her. She was a beautiful women. Just a beautiful woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know of any other Jewish women or girls hiding in that--

speaker: interviewee
text: There was one young woman, yeah. She was a little bit older than me. She was also in the convent. I don't really know her name. See, a lot of times you don't really want to know everybody's history. You don't want to know because somebody could talk or something. So you just almost say hello and goodbye, and that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So no one there knew that you were Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: Except Mother Superior and the priest. Because I had to go to confession-- pretend to go to confession.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe what that was like?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was horrible because the priest kept telling me stories all about how the Jews killed Jesus Christ. Until one day I told Mother Superior-- I said, you know, I'm not going to confession anymore because I don't believe that I killed Jesus Christ. She said, no, you don't have to go. And I think she had a talk with him. I got so dry there.
